There was a blind man during the time of the Messenger of Allah [SAW] who had an Umm Walad by whom he had two sons. Finally, I mentioned your name and she slandered you, so I went and got a dagger which I thrust into her stomach, and leaned on it until I killed her. raw_content:
Thanks for covering this story! Zara's case highlights school bullying in Malaysia to a whole new level. I actually don't mind those clout-chasers because they're spreading more awareness on the matter. We didn't do it for Zulfarhan and his case unfortunately was quickly forgotten. Malaysian schools have A LOT of bullying cases, they're just mostly silenced or brushed off as kids being kids. I was fortunate to be in a considerably normal class, but once in my final year of high school, I decided to join dormitory just for experience before college.

The first day I was approached by a group of girls who started friendly, but suddenly went through my stuff without my permission, under the pretext of helping me sort my stuff. I was shocked and started trembling. They pointed out my trembles, laughed it off and tried to calm me down by saying they were just intrigued that I joined in my final year, and told me to follow them close. So yeah I unknowingly joined the bully group. The first month I was there, there were a few moments when I made mistakes and got yelled by juniors. When I turned around and tried to apologise, the junior who just recognised me as a senior, would frantically say sorry first and ran. I told another friend (outside the bully group) and asked about it, and she advised me not to be obvious at being nice to juniors because the group doesn't like it. Long story short, I left dormitory after 2 months. Didn't even say bye to the group. Just one day while everyone's at the mosque for afternoon prayer, I packed my bag and left. I met a teacher on my way out, he adored me so he was surprised that I left, but I think he had some guesses so he didn't say much, just wished me good luck.

Im in my 20s now and did grow up in religious boarding school in Malaysia, Kuala Lumpur to be precise. I'd say my experience was quite nice especially regarding friendships. We were taught to always take care of our ukhwah and i think thats really good, it really helps me alot. 

some of the teachers are not nice and i cant fit in in with my classmates thats always resulted with me hanging out anywhere else as long as its not my class during night prep class but that was when im older, 16,17 so i can handle it. there were times i cried calling my parents to pick me up because i hate being there. the exhaustion waking up at 5am, attending Quran reading at 6.30am, finishing class at 4pm, another reading Quran class at 7 until 9pm, then night prep class until 11pm. The earliest you can sleep was around 12am but then you need to do your laundry, iron your clothes, there are still some homework thats unfinished, yes, its exhausting. 

however, i can handle that, because its not only me, all of us do the same thing so we bonded after that, its fine everything's fine. Then when i turned 16, new classmates, new faces, suddenly i hate it. All of my closed friends are not in it and then theres this one creep who always make fun of me, calling me 'Endon' (derogatory word for Indonesian) because my parents are Indonesians and Im Indonesian by blood. I can handle that but what i cannot accept was that the teacher and classmates that I know are also Indonesians by blood laugh at me too. It sucks. I didnt do anything, why are they treating me like that? the boy will rummage through my bag, use a permanent marker and scribble my water bottle, make fun of my voice that then the others will laugh along including teachers mind you. I hate him. I hate that hes the favourite among teachers. I hate that the girls in my class likes him. That creep that looks like an ape?? That creep that goes through people's bag? Hate it. 

Thankfully my closed friends are mostly nice, and i got plenty of them. Some teachers are still nice too and thats where im experiencing alot of stuffs that i wouldnt dare to do on my own. Camping, jungle trekking at night, and lots of camps lol. Its fun, i travel a lot too when i was there all thanks to my teachers that requested it because they knows we are exhausted and needed a break. But that creepy ape looking guy, i'll never forgive you.

I rarely comment on videos and such, but this case really sparked my interest. As a Malaysian high schooler attenting public school, bullying cases are extremely common, especially in boarding schools. It's saddening that these cases aren't highlighted enough, often downplayed and dismissed with a blind eye. I have a maternal uncle (he's Chinese) who attended boarding school in his teens, and he was bullied because of his race and his inexperience with the national language. It's possible I'm being paranoid, or explaining things from a single point of view, but I believe boarding schools tend to have a higher rate of bullying cases than public schools. And of course, racial discrimination is common, especially in Malaysia. I'm not saying this as a way to put down our country. I strongly believe there IS unity, but it's not always the case. Different people have different experiences (I have an kind Malay friend, Chinese neighbors, and an Indian best friend).

Truly, it wasn't until Zara's case that shed light to bullying cases in schools. Many have even voiced their stories in social media, and it pains me to know how common it is, regardless of any form of bullying. Thank you Megan for reuploading this video for the third time! I'm a fan of your videos, and knowing that you covered such a case fills me with gladness. Zara deserves justice, and your voice for spreading word is admirable!

raw_content: Parents and young people everywhere must educate themselves on the behavioral phenomenon of a psycho(path). These psychos are not mad - they simply lack the ability to feel what other people can feel ..... as in empathy. The ability to empathize is what makes us uniquely human. It means that we are able to feel how other people are feeling whether they are sad, happy or painful. Psychos can't feel these emotions. And so, they can stab, twist and turn a knife in a victim while at the same time wishing the victim have a good day. Thankfully, in any society, there are only a very slim chance of such people being born at any one time. Yet, they constitute anywhere between 0.5 to 1.5 percent of the population at any given time. Therefore, they do exist in any society and it pays for individuals to know how to recognize them. To this end, Professor Emeritus Robert Hare of UBC spent his entire life studying this phenomenon. It is important for everyone to read up on his work, and especially for young singles, if they do not wish to select a psycho for marriage. Search on google his name or his work, " without conscience", and begin learning the basics of psychopathy. raw_content:
I was bullied before po from elementary and high school (students and a teacher). When I was in elementary. I wasn't the brightest, let alone the most athletic, I was just a normal hormonal young girl na late sa class and also hindi gumagawa ng assignments. I was a rebel, but not for a cause. There was this one group that would always vebally bully some students for their actions and appearances. I was a constant victim of their bullying and also to the point where I always palpitated at yuyuko sa mga classes namin. It messed up my confidence and ability to talk to people without getting paranoid as to what I would say. I can smile and nod, pero naging pipi ako if someone would talk to me and ask questions. Also had a teacher which would enable these types of bullying. She was reported by a student after I graduated and nag-iba na yung tone niya as per my younger brother.

After 10 years I started to comeback, become more confident and even do reportings without being afraid. I'm not going to compare my life sa mga previous bullies ko, but it gave me somewhat a traumatic experience growing up.

raw_content:
I was bullied a lot especially noong elem ako. Usually ung mga bullies are either may kapit, mga troublemaker and meron rin ung time na binully ako ng top ng class namin.

Usually reason nila is either deep reason or minsan trip lang nila. Sinsaksaktan ako, verbal abuse at sisirain pa gamit ko.

Di ko noon masabi sabi noon na nabubully ako kasi mahiyain ako. (Another reason they target me. Easy at hindi umiimik.)

This cause me to transfer a lot of school pero kahit saan ako magpunta meron parin mga bullies. Always targeting those who are alone, quiet and weak.

Day care until senior high I was bullied. Sa college, chismosa na walang valid evidence nalang naranasan ko. Ung mahilig manira ng reputation kahit wala ka namang ginawang masama sa kanila.

All in all, I just tried my best to ignore them. Kasi wala noon akong mapagsasabihan. Kasi sinabi ko one time sa teacher di siya naniwala. Sabi niya napakaweak ko daw at bat ko  ba kasi hinahayaan na mabully ako.

Sa mga nabubully diyan, I wish you guys have someone to lean on. Matatakbuhan para matulungan ka. Be strong. Laban lang.

raw_content:
oh yeas! I was bullied nung grade 5. Naka short hair ako noon like boy cut short hair. Then, habang patagal ng patagal nagiging pugad na sya ng ibon yung ichura which resulted into bullying. Ang laging asar sakin is may kuto kasi nga nag curl up na yung hair ko dahil sa iksi. Worse is someone sa class mag nagsabi na pag nag exchange gift daw at ako ang nabunot eh ireregalo nila ay Shampoo. shet. grabe. whole grade 5 ko ata nag ka depression ako at walang month na di ako umiyak sa pang bubully dahil sa buhok ko. 

tapos noong same year rin na yun nagka crush ako sa classmate ko, nung nalaman nya na crush ko sya, umiwas na sya sakin ng sobra. grabe. haha... 

Never ako nag sumbong sa magulang ko kasi alam ko paano sila magalit and as a growing teen nakakahiya ipatwag ang magulang.

and here I am kilala ko pa rin sila at wala na kong pake. that thing that happened resulted into my introversion and my escape is drawing, that's also where I started to practice yung drawing skills ko. And it resulted into me advocating to anti-bullying sa school.

Mind you this is from a Catholic school. Kaya di ako naniniwala na di lahat matitino sa Catholic school.

raw_content:
I was bullied before from elementary to highschool and yung bully na yun? iisang tao lang. I let her take the days na sana eh magiging memorable and happiest moments ko sa buhay. imagine ilang taon lang ako nun 8-14??? yung way niya ng pang bubully is physically and emotionally, sinasaktan nila ako. yes "nila" may mga alipores pa siya. dumating pa kami sa point na hindi ako kakausapin ng buong section kasi takot sila dun sa bully, wala akong friends from elem-highschool na masasabi ko talagang best buddy ko. I did NOTHING. i tolerate their wrongdoings. i endured it ALONE. I  never told ANYONE, i never cried in front of ANYONE. And it affects me mentally. Hanggang ngayon hirap akong mag tiwala sa mga tao, hindi ko alam kung totoo ba yung pinapakita nila sakin. And dun ko natutunan yung salitang "you deserve what you tolerate"

Hanggang ngayon naiinggit ako sa mga taong nagkukwento ng highschool life nila, the best daw ang highschool days. Pero hindi ako maka relate. Kapag tinatanong nila ako kung anong best memory ko nung highschool, wala akong maalala. Ni minsan hindi din ako umattend ng school reunion namin. Hindi man siguro kasing fresh yung sugat noon, habang buhay ko na sigurong dadalahin yung peklat sa pangyayari na yun.

KAYA PLEASE, KAPAG MAY NAGTATRY MAMBULLY SA INYO STAND FOR YOURSELF. YES MAHIRAP, NAKAKA TAKOT PERO WAG NIYO HAYAAN NA MADALA NIYO YUNG TAKOT NIYO HABANG BUHAY.

AND PLEASE KAMUSTAHIN NIYO PALAGI ANG MGA KAIBIGAN, ANAK AT KUNG SINO MAN SA PAMILYA NIYO. Let them know na di sila nag iisa.

To everyone, please know that YOU'RE NEVER ALONE.

MENTAL HEALTH MATTERS, NO TO BULLYING!!!!

Banyak orang mendengar berita bahawa kerajaan mencadangkan sekatan penggunaan media sosial yang dihadkan kepada umur 16 tahun, dan adalah wajar bahawa kanak-kanak dan remaja menyatakan kekecewaan terhadap perkara ini.

Kerajaan mesti FAHAM bahawa sebab mengapa golongan muda menyatakan kekecewaan atau rasa frustrasi terhadap sekatan media sosial bukan kerana mereka menolak disiplin atau menolak keselamatan, tetapi kerana mereka merasakan bahawa larangan tersebut mengabaikan kepentingan emosi dan sosial yang dimiliki platform-platform ini dalam kehidupan mereka.

Media sosial telah menjadi tempat untuk perhubungan, kreativiti, dan rasa memiliki bagi generasi hari ini. Oleh itu, isu sebenar bukanlah penolakan terhadap langkah-langkah keselamatan, tetapi rasa bahawa suara mereka tidak didengari dalam keputusan yang membentuk dunia sosial mereka.

Apa yang kita semua perlukan, sekali lagi, bukanlah TINDAKAN SEGERA, tetapi PEMAHAMAN YANG RASIONAL antara kerajaan dan golongan muda. Apa yang benar-benar penting bukanlah KEPANTASAN PELARANGAN, tetapi PEMBENTUKAN DIALOG DAN KEPERCAYAAN BERSAMA antara pembuat dasar dan generasi yang akan mewarisi masa depan.

Hanya dengan pemahaman sudah memadai  kerana ia merupakan asas bagi individu untuk membentuk kepercayaan umum yang membawa kepada membuat keputusan yang bijak!

Many people are hearing of the news that the government proposing restrictions over the use of social media limiting to the age of 16, and it makes sense that kids and adolescents express disappointment with this.

raw_content:
At least, they didn't blanket all accusations on violent media. It's NOT and has NEVER been the answer so many think it is. Maybe we're finally seeing that evidence become undeniable, but I'm not holding my breath.
There's a pattern to those who ARE PREDISPOSED TO VIOLENCE where it comes to media, including websites. Like almost everyone else, it's just curiosity, so they hot the sites rarely, but return... and the pace picks up as their ner out hesitation gives in to fascination.
This is where it shifts. For most normal kids, they meet their curiosity and move on... some grow t9 fascination, but it, too, is short lived, and they find other things but may return for newer developments... new incidents and the like. Trolls carry on a fascination with trolling, and their mentality is questionable at best... and finally, the violently unstable DWELL beyond a short term fascination, becoming obsessed with the content of choice.
We bitch about the toxicity of Randoms, but don't bat an eye at Swifties, who even make whole identities of their obsession, but they are NO MORE NORMAL than someone obsessed with Columbine.
There's also the fixation of note taking and diagrams scratched together from film, anime, and video games. Normal people aren't taking notes while they play, aside from that obvious numerical code planted obscurely for reference at some late Level you won't  get back from... certainly NOT like this kid.
Rather than pushing regulations, which will ONLY EVER punish the creators and chase the lunatics to underground sites in TOR or worse, MAYBE we should be nudging regulations more to quietly observing traffic and send probable cases the advertisements for easier access to council, or even encourage parents to log whatever their computers do, regardless of security protocols, just so they CAN better monitor their kids online... if there's no pass code to disable logging, they'll have a hell of a time figuring out how to disable it (if they even notice being logged)...
I've been and seen and played or done practically every dangerous and dubious thing people have tried to blame for violence ir whatever else, and NOT EVEN ONCE did anything seem to be pushing me toward a violent outburst let alone the encouragement of violence or murder as a problem resolution...
The kid was f***ed before he even watched Death Note or much of anything else. 
Online there are subcultures of these serial killer and  murderer wannabes, but we (Humans and authorities) NEED to face the fact that these sites and the traffic is a SYMPTOM of systemic problems and NOT the core of the issue, itself.
Only then will anyone be able to start efforts toward a resolution.

raw_content:
Malaysian here:

This is the closest our school system ever got to a Columbine type of incident; although it could gone way worse had a g*n been used. With that said, it is terrible tragedy that has befallen that young girl & no young person ever should go through that. 

And indeed, it is a shock to Malaysian society given our tough g*n laws, and the assumption that our schools are "safe" from violent crime. Well, that all came shattering down the moment that young girl died that day on Oct 14th. 

If anyone has seen the Adolescence Netflix series, there were eerie parallels to the SMK BUD4 case.

The manifesto note left by the perpetrator clearly shows that the kid was radicalised by the content he consumed online. Not excusing the kid from the crime committed, but it seems the adults in his life - especially the parents - have failed him. It all starts at home, the teachers at school can only do so much. 

When I was teen in the 1990's, Internet accessibility was limited & smartphones weren't as prevalent. One of the few things my folks will monitor me on is who I am hanging out with in school & whether my academic grades were affected. There wasn't much worry about the dangers of the online world. 

One of the concerning fall outs after this, is the Ministry of Education ( MOE ) & the Multimedia Commission ( MCMC ) taking a "shortcut route" to tighten the ban on the Internet. This is only a short term stop gap measure. However, a recent proposal has suggested that Malaysian teenagers under the age of 16 be banned from social media usage.

To address this issue of early youth radicalisation & the dangers of the digital world, it requires a more holistic approach. Good values need to be promoted and inculcated at home by the parents with regular monitoring of their child's online activity & knowing who are the friends their kids are hanging out with.  

Teachers can further reinforce the learned values in school, and this will require a huge revamp to the Pendidikan Moral ( Moral Studies ) subject. What the MOE can propose is a Pendidikan Sosial ( Social Studies ) segment that can teach children about things such as consent, bad touch / good touch, cause & effect of bad behavior / crime, reward good behavior shown, a school visit to a prison or rehabilitation centre, online dangers - cat fishing, youth radicalisation through KOL / influencers. 

It will take a few generations to get this inculcated into society, but the long term pay off will be a reduction in crime - be it juvenile or adult crimes.

I was in the same batch with Zulfarhan in high school. We were never in the same class, and I never talked to him, but I always see him as a nice, quiet guy who will never try to be mean to others. We were in an all-boys school (MSAB) and most of the boys in his class were loud, but not him. Everyone seems to get along with him as well, and he always smile at others, even to those he doesn't know.

I'm saying this because the excuse they used to beat him up is just that, an excuse. They went to see a bomoh (shaman) and completely believed the bomoh's accusation despite having no proof or witness to the theft. The bullying culture in Malaysia, also known as ragging, has been a practice done especially in a hostel life. 

Another student from UPNM was also accused in killing Zulfarhan but upon further investigation, it was found that he was innocent and framed because he was seen talking to Zulfarhan. He was released and stated that he want to continue his study again after been given the second chance.

After the first court session gave them 18 years instead of death penalty, the 6 killers were seen smiling and laughing as they leave the court despite the crime they had committed, showing absolutely no remorse. Now that they are sentenced to death penalty, it is very satisfying to see their new reaction when leaving the court.

In an interview with his parents, they stated that only one of the bullies' parents came to apologise to them for what their son had done. The other parents avoided seeing them and made it seems like Zulfarhan was the one in the wrong.

I've followed this news since I first heard of it. I'm glad that the parents fought for justice, and the killers are sentenced to death. I talked about this case with my 2 other friends from MSAB and all of us are glad that the killers finally get their judgement.

I think people are mistaken. This was never a bullying incident. The laptop was missing for a few hours before one of their peers consulted his father who claims to have mystic powers on the incident. The father then contacted the person whose laptop was missing and then concluded that the victim was the perpetrator. The victim was woken up by the person and then taken to another room for interrogation. No prior resentment, acts, or planning to target the victim. It didn't start small then became worst. The others had no stake in the initial stolen laptop case, they simply were convinced that the victim had been the thief and acted on that assumption in the worst possible way, mob justice.

Maybe one could argue that the reason they acted the way they did came from the 'culture' of bullying where they all learned how to inflict pain on others, but bear in mind, there are other cases of mob justice from complete civilians that ended very gruesomely. Notably, there was a case where a driver was caught, tied up, then beaten to death by a group of riders working for food delivery companies. You don't have to go through or learn from bullying to know that being hit hurts, that being burned hurts. I think calling this bullying, implies that the victim's death was an unintentional consequence.

i think i disagree,

There was 90 bloody burn marks all over the victim's body, including on his private parts, over the course of, at minimum, 1 hour. That's more than 30 seconds at a time the iron was placed directly on the victim's body. Consecutively. His death is not an accident no matter what the perpetrators say. raw_content:
Really hard one to watch.  I grew up in Borneo and attended a religious school in the mornings before standard school, alongside my brother.  One relevant point is that my brother and I are mixed race, and this was the early 90s.  I was "lucky" in that I was mostly ignored in classes because not only did I not understand the classes (we were raised in a predominantly English speaking household and had mediocre Malay knowledge and no real Arabic knowledge), but I was also quiet, so I think teachers wrote me off as dumb (I was kept back two years, after all).  My brother, whilst similar in language skills to me, was a real cheeky prankster and drew attention to himself.  There was a mixed prayer training class that we were both attending in the general hall, where we were being taught the stances for the Islamic prayer.  In Malay, I remember the instructor telling the boys to position their elbows properly in the head down kneeling position, and said the equivalent of "You're praying to God, not sniffing for food like dogs!"  My brother, bless him, started barking and got a laugh from the students, but he was brought up in front of everyone and punished.  

I don't know how long after this incident it was, but our dad came into the school one day to talk to the principal about something, and he found my brother in the teachers' lounge, massaging the instructor's feet as "discipline".  To say our dad went O F F  was an understatement.  Unfortunately, we couldn't be pulled out from the school because it was the only one serving our district, I think, and our family would have been in trouble if we stopped attending, but that teacher was quietly disciplined, and we eventually did end up moving to Europe not long after anyway for a variety of reasons.
